refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. A system can correctly measure energy_per_ton without guaranteeing the target is met—that depends on operational practices, equipment tuning, and worksite conditions. By refining to focus on measurement and reporting capability, the FR becomes solution-neutral (does not prescribe how energy is reduced) and atomic (single obligation). Achievement of E_target becomes a verification criterion applied to measured data, not a functional requirement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh, fuel volume, or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved, distance, cycles) during operation, and produces a report or dashboard showing calculated energy_per_ton. Verify the calculation is correct and traceable to source telemetry.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log energy consumption data during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall acquire and log work output data (e.g., mass moved, distance, cycle count) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate energy_per_ton as the ratio of total energy consumed to total work output over a defined evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall report energy_per_ton and supporting metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality) in a human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio asynchronously via independent metering and data aggregation layer.
DP2: Embed energy sensors in prime mover; embed load sensors on transport mechanism; fuse signals in real-time controller; compute and log energy_per_ton locally with periodic reporting.

DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs (manual or automated pump records) and work output via independent weighbridge or load cell array; reconcile via batch data pipeline with human-in-loop validation.

DP4: Deploy distributed sensor network (energy, load, position) with edge compute nodes; synchronize via time-stamped event stream; aggregate and compute metrics in centralized analytics platform with configurable evaluation windows.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio asynchronously via independent metering and data aggregation layer.

potential_sources: Energy metering and work output metering remain logically decoupled—each can be replaced or upgraded independently without affecting the other.
Asynchronous computation via data aggregation layer decouples measurement timing from reporting, reducing real-time coupling.
Risk: If metering devices share a single communication bus or power supply, physical coupling could emerge at IM layer—mitigated by explicit module boundary separation.

Risk: Acceptance criteria tie energy_per_ton to evaluation period; if period definition is ambiguous, coupling between FR and acceptance criteria could propagate to DP. Recommend explicit period definition in FR refinement.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low functional coupling because energy and work are measured independently and combined only in the aggregation layer. This supports modularity and testability. DP2 exhibits tighter coupling (real-time fusion in controller); DP3 introduces human-in-loop coupling; DP4 introduces temporal synchronization coupling across distributed nodes.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel or electricity) can be metered at a single, well-defined point. Risk: If energy is supplied from multiple sources or stored in batteries with variable efficiency, metering at source may not capture true consumption. Mitigation: Clarify energy boundary in FR.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) is measurable at point of delivery. Risk: If material is lost, spilled, or transferred mid-journey, measurement at delivery may not reflect actual work performed. Mitigation: Define 'work output' precisely (e.g., net tonnage delivered, gross tonnage loaded, or tonnage at intermediate checkpoints).

Assumption: Evaluation period is fixed and known in advance. Risk: If period is variable or overlaps with operational transitions, energy_per_ton may be distorted by startup/shutdown transients. Mitigation: Specify evaluation period definition and handling of boundary conditions in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Data aggregation layer can reliably correlate energy and work measurements across time. Risk: If clocks drift or data is lost, correlation may fail. Mitigation: Implement time-synchronization and data validation in aggregation layer design.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints on sensor placement or data collection. Risk: If safety policies restrict access to energy source or work output measurement points, DP1 may be infeasible. Mitigation: Validate sensor placement against safety plan early.

Assumption: E_target is achievable with current equipment and operational practices. Risk: If E_target is set below baseline performance, DP1 will measure non-compliance but cannot drive improvement without additional FR for optimization. Mitigation: Baseline energy_per_ton before finalizing E_target.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output, where energy consumption is measured at the primary energy source (fuel tank or electrical supply point), work output is measured as net tonnage delivered to the designated endpoint, and energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_tonnage_delivered is computed and reported for each evaluation period (defined as [specify: shift, day, week, or project phase]). The solution shall support acceptance criteria: measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (specify units, e.g., kWh/ton or MJ/ton) over the agreed evaluation period, with data validation and reconciliation procedures defined in the worksite operational plan.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy efficiency metering and data aggregation layer that instruments fuel/electricity input at source, work output at delivery point, and computes energy ratio asynchronously across the fleet.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw energy consumption data (fuel/electricity) from Vehicle and work output metrics (tonnage, distance) from point-of-delivery sensors.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Publishes computed energy efficiency ratios and aggregated performance analytics back to Vehicle and Teleoperation for visibility.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Asynchronous data transport for metering streams and computed results; decouples real-time vehicle operations from ratio computation latency.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: NGHE Solution is explicitly a data aggregation and analytics layer designed for fleet-wide efficiency monitoring. Independent metering, asynchronous computation, and ratio derivation are core to its charter. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry and Network Link interfaces leak out; Vehicle and Teleoperation remain decoupled from computation logic.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a mobile asset, not a data aggregation platform. Placing metering logic and asynchronous ratio computation in Vehicle creates tight coupling to individual asset lifecycle, complicates firmware updates, and forces redundant computation across fleet. High diffusion: requires Vehicle to export raw telemetry, coordinate with external aggregation, and manage asynchronous callbacks.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop control interface, not an analytics backend. Placing metering and ratio computation here conflates operational control with efficiency analysis, violates separation of concerns, and creates latency sensitivity in a user-facing system. High diffusion: requires Teleoperation to manage asynchronous data streams and expose analytics to external consumers.

Poor fit: Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and SafetyAlert generation, not energy efficiency metering. Placing metering logic here conflates safety-critical functions with non-critical analytics, complicates certification, and introduces unrelated data dependencies. High diffusion: requires Safety Services to ingest and process energy data orthogonal to its safety mandate.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (Energy Efficiency Analytics & Metering Team)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (independent metering, asynchronous aggregation, and ratio computation) is a fleet-level analytics function that belongs in a dedicated, non-real-time data layer. NGHE Solution is the natural home because it is already chartered as a solution-level aggregation platform. Placing this DP in NGHE Solution keeps energy instrumentation logic centralized, avoids duplication across vehicles, decouples computation from vehicle firmware, and allows asynchronous processing without impacting vehicle or teleoperation responsiveness.
containment_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface must reliably stream raw energy and work-output data from Vehicle and field sensors; if Telemetry is lossy or delayed, ratio computation becomes unreliable. Mitigation: define Telemetry SLA for energy metering streams (e.g., max latency, min completeness).

Risk: Asynchronous computation in NGHE Solution may lag real-time vehicle operations; if Teleoperation or Safety Services require immediate energy feedback, they cannot depend on NGHE Solution ratios. Mitigation: clearly document that NGHE Solution provides post-hoc analytics, not real-time control inputs.

Risk: Vehicle must instrument fuel/electricity at source; if Vehicle firmware does not reliably report consumption, NGHE Solution cannot compute accurate ratios. Mitigation: define Vehicle telemetry contract (energy data format, frequency, accuracy) as a prerequisite.

Risk: Work output instrumentation at point of delivery (e.g., scale, GPS) is external to Vehicle and NGHE Solution; if delivery-point sensors fail or are not integrated, ratio computation is incomplete. Mitigation: establish data integration contract with delivery-point systems (e.g., via Telemetry interface).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and sufficiently addresses FR: independent metering of energy input (fuel/electricity) at source and work output (tonnage) at delivery point, with asynchronous ratio computation, logically solves the requirement to measure and report energy_per_ton. DP is solution-agnostic and does not over-specify implementation details.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface is a critical bridge: if raw energy and work-output data streams are lossy, delayed, or incomplete, DP1 computation becomes unreliable. Vehicle firmware must reliably instrument fuel/electricity; delivery-point sensors must reliably report tonnage. Mitigation: formalize Telemetry SLA (max latency, min completeness, data format contract).

Asynchronous computation in NGHE Solution introduces latency: if Teleoperation or Safety Services require immediate energy feedback for real-time control or hazard response, they cannot depend on NGHE Solution ratios. Mitigation: document NGHE Solution as post-hoc analytics layer; establish separate real-time energy monitoring if needed.

Vehicle firmware dependency: Vehicle must reliably export energy consumption data; if firmware is unreliable or updates break telemetry contract, NGHE Solution cannot compute accurate ratios. Mitigation: establish Vehicle telemetry contract as a prerequisite (energy data format, frequency, accuracy, versioning).

Delivery-point sensor integration: work output instrumentation (scale, GPS, tonnage sensors) is external to both Vehicle and NGHE Solution; if sensors fail, are not integrated, or have inconsistent data quality, ratio computation is incomplete or inaccurate. Mitigation: establish data integration contract with delivery-point systems via Telemetry interface; define sensor accuracy and availability SLAs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08) is the correct IM boundary. DP1 is a fleet-level analytics function that belongs in a dedicated, non-real-time data aggregation layer. Centralizing independent metering logic, asynchronous computation, and ratio derivation in NGHE Solution avoids duplication across vehicles, decouples computation from vehicle firmware lifecycle, and allows asynchronous processing without impacting vehicle or teleoperation responsiveness. Vehicle (0.35/0.65), Teleoperation (0.28/0.72), and Safety Services (0.22/0.78) are poor fits: they are not chartered as analytics platforms and would introduce high diffusion if DP1 logic were scattered across them. Ownership candidate is correct: Energy Efficiency Analytics & Metering Team.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion `energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target` is measurable and verifiable if: (1) Vehicle reliably instruments energy_used (fuel/electricity consumed) and exports via Telemetry; (2) delivery-point sensors reliably measure ton_moved and export via Telemetry; (3) NGHE Solution computes the ratio asynchronously and reports results with timestamps and data quality metadata. Verification requires end-to-end traceability from energy source through Vehicle telemetry, delivery-point sensors, Telemetry interface, NGHE Solution computation, and final reporting. Feasibility is high if Telemetry SLAs and data contracts are formalized.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution IM boundary via DP1. Allocation is explicit: NGHE Solution owns independent metering instrumentation logic (via Telemetry input), asynchronous aggregation (via Network Link), and ratio computation (internal to NGHE Solution). Vehicle owns energy instrumentation at source (firmware responsibility); delivery-point systems own work output instrumentation (external responsibility). Teleoperation and Safety Services are decoupled from DP1 logic and receive only computed results via Telemetry output. Ownership and responsibility boundaries are clear.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Telemetry SLA for energy metering streams: define max latency (e.g., <5 min for batch aggregation), min completeness (e.g., >99% of energy records), data format (e.g., JSON schema for energy_consumed, timestamp, vehicle_id), and error handling (e.g., retry logic, data validation).

Establish Vehicle telemetry contract: specify energy data format (fuel consumption in liters/gallons, electricity in kWh), reporting frequency (e.g., per trip, per hour), accuracy tolerance (e.g., ±2%), and firmware versioning to ensure backward compatibility.

Establish delivery-point sensor integration contract: specify work output data format (tonnage in metric tons, GPS coordinates, timestamp), sensor accuracy (e.g., ±1% for scales), availability SLA (e.g., >99.5%), and data transport mechanism (e.g., via Telemetry interface).

Document NGHE Solution as post-hoc analytics layer: clearly state that computed energy_per_ton ratios are not real-time and cannot be used for immediate vehicle control or safety decisions. If real-time energy feedback is required, establish a separate real-time monitoring mechanism.

Define data quality and traceability requirements: NGHE Solution must report energy_per_ton with metadata (e.g., number of records aggregated, data completeness %, computation timestamp, sensor accuracy) to enable verification against acceptance criterion E_target.

Establish monitoring and alerting for Telemetry interface health: if energy or work-output data streams degrade, NGHE Solution must alert the Energy Efficiency Analytics & Metering Team to prevent silent failures in ratio computation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. DP1 is logically sound, NGHE Solution is the correct containment boundary, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results expose critical diffusion risks and dependencies that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure architectural integrity and risk mitigation. Specifically: (1) Telemetry interface reliability is a prerequisite for DP1 correctness but is not yet a formal FR; (2) Vehicle energy instrumentation contract is external but critical; (3) delivery-point sensor integration is external but critical; (4) data quality and traceability requirements are implicit in the acceptance criterion but need explicit decomposition. These are not implementation details—they are functional requirements that must be owned and verified. Safety criticality (high) amplifies the need to decompose data reliability, traceability, and error handling into explicit FRs before proceeding to detailed design. Stopping here would leave critical dependencies unmanaged; revising the current level would obscure the architectural dependencies. Decomposing into sub-FRs allows each dependency to be assigned ownership, verified, and monitored.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Reliably instrument and export energy consumption data (fuel/electricity) from Vehicle at source with defined accuracy, frequency, and format, such that Vehicle telemetry contract is satisfied and energy_used is traceable to source.

FR1.2: Reliably measure and export work output data (tonnage, GPS, timestamp) from delivery-point sensors with defined accuracy and availability, such that ton_moved is traceable to delivery point and sensor SLA is met.

FR1.3: Transport energy and work-output data streams from Vehicle and delivery-point sensors to NGHE Solution via Telemetry interface with defined latency, completeness, and error handling, such that DP1 computation receives complete and timely inputs.

FR1.4: Compute energy_per_ton ratio asynchronously from independent metering inputs (energy_used, ton_moved) and report results with data quality metadata (completeness %, aggregation count, computation timestamp, sensor accuracy), such that acceptance criterion energy_per_ton ≤ E_target is verifiable and traceable.

FR1.5: Monitor Telemetry interface health and data stream quality in real-time, detect degradation or loss of energy/work-output data, and alert Energy Efficiency Analytics & Metering Team to prevent silent failures in ratio computation.

revise.why: DP1 is logically correct but incomplete as a standalone design parameter. It must be refined to explicitly include: (1) independent metering instrumentation at source (Vehicle) and delivery point (external sensors); (2) Telemetry interface as a critical bridge with defined SLA; (3) asynchronous aggregation and computation in NGHE Solution with data quality reporting; (4) monitoring and alerting for Telemetry health. Revised DP1 should read: 'Implement independent metering of energy input at Vehicle source (via firmware instrumentation and telemetry export) and work output at delivery point (via external sensors and data integration), transport both streams via Telemetry interface with defined SLA (latency, completeness, format), compute energy_per_ton asynchronously in NGHE Solution with data quality metadata, and monitor Telemetry interface health with alerting.' This revision makes the DP explicit about dependencies and ownership boundaries.

assumptions: Vehicle firmware can reliably instrument and export energy consumption data (fuel/electricity) with ±2% accuracy and <5 min latency.
Delivery-point sensors (scales, GPS, tonnage instruments) exist, are integrated, and can reliably measure work output with ±1% accuracy and >99.5% availability.
Telemetry interface (Network Link) can transport energy and work-output data streams with <5 min latency and >99% completeness.
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity to aggregate and compute energy_per_ton ratios asynchronously without impacting real-time vehicle or teleoperation systems.
Energy Efficiency Analytics & Metering Team has ownership and operational responsibility for NGHE Solution and can respond to Telemetry health alerts.
Safety Services and Teleoperation do not require real-time energy feedback for immediate control or hazard response; post-hoc analytics via NGHE Solution is acceptable for their use cases.
open_risks: Vehicle firmware energy instrumentation may be unreliable, inaccurate, or break due to firmware updates; mitigation requires formal Vehicle telemetry contract and versioning strategy.
Delivery-point sensors may not be integrated, may have inconsistent data quality, or may fail; mitigation requires formal sensor integration contract and SLA.
Telemetry interface may introduce latency, data loss, or incomplete records; mitigation requires formal Telemetry SLA and monitoring.

NGHE Solution computation may be delayed or asynchronous, preventing real-time energy feedback for vehicle control or safety decisions; mitigation requires clear documentation that NGHE Solution is post-hoc analytics and separate real-time monitoring if needed.
Data quality degradation may be silent (e.g., sensors fail but data is not reported); mitigation requires explicit monitoring and alerting (FR1.5).
Traceability from energy source through computation to final reporting may be lost if data quality metadata is not captured; mitigation requires explicit data quality reporting (FR1.4).
evidence_needed: Vehicle telemetry contract: energy data format, reporting frequency, accuracy tolerance, firmware versioning strategy.
Delivery-point sensor integration contract: work output data format, sensor accuracy, availability SLA, data transport mechanism.
Telemetry SLA: max latency, min completeness, data format (JSON schema), error handling (retry logic, validation).
NGHE Solution design: asynchronous computation logic, data quality metadata capture, reporting format.
Monitoring and alerting design: Telemetry interface health metrics, alert thresholds, escalation procedures.

End-to-end traceability verification: trace energy_used from Vehicle source through Telemetry to NGHE Solution computation; trace ton_moved from delivery-point sensors through Telemetry to NGHE Solution computation.
Acceptance test plan: verify energy_per_ton ≤ E_target with defined data quality thresholds and traceability metadata.
